annotations.label: Number

anchor.snippet: A number is a mathematical object used to count

annotations.note: Mathlib has no single overarching `Number` type; specific number systems are formalized individually.

annotations.label: Numeral system

anchor.snippet: a numeral system is used to represent any number in an organized way

annotations.note: `Nat.digits` provides representation of naturals in an arbitrary base, capturing positional numeral systems.

annotations.label: Hindu–Arabic numeral system

anchor.snippet: The most common representation is the Hindu–Arabic numeral system

annotations.note: Base-10 representation is the special case `Nat.digits 10`; the historical system itself is not formalized.

annotations.label: Transcendental number

anchor.snippet: A transcendental number is a numerical value that is not the root of a polynomial with integer coefficients

annotations.note: `Transcendental R x` is defined as `¬ IsAlgebraic R x`.

annotations.label: Hermite: e is transcendental

anchor.snippet: Hermite proved in 1873 that e is transcendental

annotations.note: No statement that `Real.exp 1` is transcendental was found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Lindemann: π is transcendental

anchor.snippet: Lindemann proved in 1882 that π is transcendental

annotations.note: Only the analytical part of Lindemann–Weierstrass is formalized; transcendence of π itself is not yet stated.

annotations.label: Uncountability of transcendentals

anchor.snippet: Cantor showed that the set of all real numbers is uncountably infinite but the set of all algebraic numbers is countably infinite

annotations.note: Combined with `Cardinal.mk_real = 𝔠` (Mathlib.Analysis.Real.Cardinality), this gives uncountably many real transcendentals.

annotations.label: Irrationality of π (Lambert)

anchor.snippet: The irrationality of π was proved in 1761 by Johann Lambert

annotations.note: `irrational_pi : Irrational π` is a `simp` lemma.

annotations.label: Euler: continued fraction characterization of irrationals

anchor.snippet: proved that the irrational numbers are those numbers whose simple continued fractions is not finite

annotations.note: The continued fraction of `v` terminates iff `v` is rational, which is the same characterization.

annotations.label: Irrationality of √2 (Hippasus)

anchor.snippet: produced a (most likely geometrical) proof of the irrationality of the square root of 2

annotations.note: Stated directly as `Irrational (√2)`.

annotations.label: De Moivre's formula

anchor.snippet: De Moivre's formula (1730) states

annotations.note: Provides `(cos z + sin z * I) ^ n = cos (n*z) + sin (n*z) * I`.

annotations.label: Euler's formula

anchor.snippet: while Euler's formula of complex analysis (1748) gave us

annotations.note: `cos x + sin x * I = exp (x * I)` is Euler's formula.

annotations.label: Euler's identity

anchor.snippet: A special case of this formula yields Euler's identity

annotations.note: `exp (π * I) = -1`, equivalent to Euler's identity.

annotations.label: Fundamental theorem of algebra (Gauss)

anchor.snippet: Gauss provided the first generally accepted proof of the fundamental theorem of algebra

annotations.note: Every nonconstant complex polynomial has a root, with `Complex.isAlgClosed` as instance.

annotations.label: Prime number (historical)

anchor.snippet: They are natural numbers that are not a product of two smaller natural numbers

annotations.note: `Nat.Prime` matches Euclid's definition.

annotations.label: Infinitude of primes (Euclid)

anchor.snippet: he proved the infinitude of the primes and the fundamental theorem of arithmetic

annotations.note: Also available as `Nat.infinite_setOf_prime`.

annotations.label: Wilson's theorem

anchor.snippet: Ibn al-Haytham discovered Wilson's theorem

annotations.note: States `Prime n ↔ ((n-1)! : ZMod n) = -1` for `n ≠ 1`.

annotations.label: Prime number theorem

anchor.snippet: Adrien-Marie Legendre conjectured the prime number theorem

annotations.note: Chebyshev-style bounds are formalized but the full PNT asymptotic π(x) ~ x/log x is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Divergence of sum of reciprocals of primes

anchor.snippet: Euler's proof that the sum of the reciprocals of the primes diverges

annotations.note: Directly states that the series of reciprocals of primes is not summable.

annotations.label: Goldbach conjecture

anchor.snippet: the Goldbach conjecture , which claims that any sufficiently large even number is the sum of two primes

annotations.note: No formal statement of Goldbach's conjecture was found in Mathlib (Mathlib's `Goldbach's theorem` reference is about Fermat numbers).

annotations.label: Riemann hypothesis

anchor.snippet: the Riemann hypothesis , formulated by Bernhard Riemann in 1859

annotations.note: Statement is formally defined as a `Prop`, though not proved.

annotations.label: Natural numbers

anchor.snippet: The most familiar numbers are the natural numbers

annotations.note: `Nat` is built into Lean core and pervasively used by Mathlib.

annotations.label: Radix / base

anchor.snippet: The radix or base is the number of unique numerical digits

annotations.note: `Nat.digits b n` gives the digit list of `n` in base `b`.

annotations.label: Peano successor representation

anchor.snippet: in Peano Arithmetic , the number 3 is represented as

annotations.note: The `Nat` type is defined inductively via `zero` and `succ`.

annotations.label: Negative integer

anchor.snippet: The negative of a positive integer is defined as a number that produces 0 when it is added to the corresponding positive integer

annotations.note: `Int` constructs negatives via `negSucc` and provides additive inverses.

annotations.label: Set of integers

anchor.snippet: When the set of negative numbers is combined with the set of natural numbers (including 0), the result is defined as the set of integers

annotations.note: `Int` is the standard integer type.

annotations.label: Integers form a ring

anchor.snippet: The set of integers forms a ring with the operations addition and multiplication

annotations.note: There is a `CommRing Int` instance (in fact `LinearOrderedCommRing`).

annotations.label: Rational number

anchor.snippet: A rational number is a number that can be expressed as a fraction with an integer numerator and a positive integer denominator

annotations.note: `Rat` carries `num : ℤ` and `den : ℕ` with `den ≠ 0`.

annotations.label: Improper fraction

anchor.snippet: then the absolute value of the fraction is greater than 1 and it is termed an improper or top-heavy fraction

annotations.note: `improper fraction` as a named concept is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Equal fractions example

anchor.snippet: Two different fractions may correspond to the same rational number

annotations.note: Mathlib normalizes rationals so equal fractions yield the same `Rat`, but no specific example lemma exists.

annotations.label: Repeating decimal

anchor.snippet: Such a decimal is called a repeating decimal

annotations.note: No formal definition of repeating decimal exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Repeating decimals are rationals

anchor.snippet: These recurring decimals, including the repetition of zeroes , denote exactly the rational numbers

annotations.note: Not stated; the closest analogue is `GenContFract.terminates_iff_rat` for continued fractions.

annotations.label: 0.999... equals 1

anchor.snippet: the same real number may have more than one decimal representation

annotations.note: The 0.999... = 1 identity is not stated as a Mathlib lemma.

annotations.label: Irrational number

anchor.snippet: These real numbers are called irrational

annotations.note: `Irrational x ↔ x ∉ range ((↑) : ℚ → ℝ)`.

annotations.label: π is irrational

anchor.snippet: A famous irrational real number is the π

annotations.note: Same `irrational_pi` lemma.

annotations.label: √2 is irrational

anchor.snippet: the square root of 2 , that is, the unique positive real number whose square is 2

annotations.note: Direct lemma.

annotations.label: Almost all reals are irrational

anchor.snippet: Almost all real numbers are irrational and therefore have no repeating patterns

annotations.note: Reals have cardinality continuum while rationals are countable, but no direct `Irrational` almost-everywhere statement was found.

annotations.label: Least upper bound property

anchor.snippet: The real numbers have an important but highly technical property called the least upper bound property

annotations.note: `ConditionallyCompleteLinearOrder ℝ` provides the LUB property via `csSup`.

annotations.label: Uniqueness of complete ordered field

anchor.snippet: It can be shown that any complete , ordered field is isomorphic to the real numbers

annotations.note: Constructs a unique `OrderRingIso` between any two conditionally complete linear ordered fields.

annotations.label: Reals not algebraically closed

anchor.snippet: The real numbers are not, however, an algebraically closed field

annotations.note: No direct `¬ IsAlgClosed ℝ` statement was found, though `Real.nonempty_algEquiv_or` and degree-2 irreducibility lemmas exist.

annotations.label: Imaginary unit

anchor.snippet: the square root of −1 is denoted by i , a symbol assigned by Leonhard Euler called the imaginary unit

annotations.note: `Complex.I` is the imaginary unit with `I^2 = -1`.

annotations.label: Complex number

anchor.snippet: complex numbers consist of all values of the form

annotations.note: `structure Complex` with `re im : ℝ`.

annotations.label: Real and imaginary parts

anchor.snippet: the real number a is called the real part and b is called the imaginary part

annotations.note: `Complex.re` and `Complex.im` are projections.

annotations.label: Imaginary / Gaussian integer

anchor.snippet: If the real part of a complex number is 0, then the number is called an imaginary number

annotations.note: Gaussian integers are `Zsqrtd (-1)`; purely imaginary numbers correspond to `Complex.re = 0`.

annotations.label: Fundamental theorem of algebra

anchor.snippet: The fundamental theorem of algebra asserts that the complex numbers form an algebraically closed field

annotations.note: Instance `IsAlgClosed ℂ` proven from `Complex.exists_root`.

annotations.label: Complex numbers form a non-ordered field

anchor.snippet: the complex numbers form a field , which is complete , but unlike the real numbers, it is not ordered

annotations.note: `ℂ` is a complete field (`NormedField ℂ`), and no linear order instance exists; non-orderability is not stated explicitly.

annotations.label: Even and odd numbers

anchor.snippet: An even number is an integer that is "evenly divisible" by two

annotations.note: `Even` and `Odd` are defined for general semirings; specializes to integers.

annotations.label: Form of odd and even numbers

anchor.snippet: Any odd number n may be constructed by the formula

annotations.note: `Odd a ↔ ∃ b, a = 2 * b + 1` and `even_iff_exists_two_mul` provide the canonical forms.

annotations.label: Parity of products

anchor.snippet: The product of an even number with an integer is another even number

annotations.note: `Even.mul_left`, `Even.mul_right`, and `Odd.mul` cover product parities.

annotations.label: Prime number

anchor.snippet: A prime number , often shortened to just prime , is an integer greater than 1 that is not the product of two smaller positive integers

annotations.note: Standard definition.

annotations.label: Mersenne primes

anchor.snippet: A special class are the Mersenne primes , which are prime numbers of the form

annotations.note: `mersenne p = 2^p - 1`; primality is tested by the Lucas–Lehmer residue.

annotations.label: Fundamental theorem of arithmetic

anchor.snippet: this proven claim is called the fundamental theorem of arithmetic

annotations.note: Naturals form a `UniqueFactorizationMonoid`.

annotations.label: Algebraic number

anchor.snippet: Algebraic numbers are those that are a solution to a polynomial equation with integer coefficients

annotations.note: `IsAlgebraic R x` is defined as existence of a nonzero polynomial in `R[X]` with `x` as a root.

annotations.label: Irrational number (classification)

anchor.snippet: Real numbers that are not rational numbers are called irrational numbers

annotations.note: Same `Irrational` predicate as above.

annotations.label: Transcendental number (classification)

anchor.snippet: Complex numbers which are not algebraic are called transcendental numbers

annotations.note: `Transcendental R x := ¬ IsAlgebraic R x` covers the complex case.

annotations.label: Algebraic integer

anchor.snippet: The algebraic numbers that are solutions of a monic polynomial equation with integer coefficients are called algebraic integers

annotations.note: `IsIntegral ℤ x` is precisely the algebraic-integer condition; `NumberField.RingOfIntegers` collects them.

annotations.label: Period

anchor.snippet: A period is a complex number that can be expressed as an integral of an algebraic function over an algebraic domain

annotations.note: Kontsevich–Zagier periods are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Exponential period

anchor.snippet: This gives another countable ring: the exponential periods

annotations.note: Exponential periods are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Constructible numbers

anchor.snippet: the constructible numbers are those complex numbers whose real and imaginary parts can be constructed using straightedge and compass

annotations.note: No `Constructible` predicate or straightedge-and-compass field was found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Computable number

anchor.snippet: A computable number , also known as recursive number , is a real number such that there exists an algorithm

annotations.note: Mathlib has `Computable` for functions but no notion of computable real number.

annotations.label: Computable numbers form a real closed field

anchor.snippet: The computable numbers are stable for all usual arithmetic operations

annotations.note: No computable-reals field structure exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: No equality algorithm for computables

anchor.snippet: there is no algorithm for testing the equality of two computable numbers

annotations.note: Not formalized; depends on a missing notion of computable real.

annotations.label: Cardinality of computable numbers

anchor.snippet: The set of computable numbers has the same cardinality as the natural numbers

annotations.note: Not formalized; computable reals are absent.

annotations.label: p-adic numbers

anchor.snippet: The p -adic numbers may have infinitely long expansions to the left of the decimal point

annotations.note: `Padic p` is the field `ℚ_[p]` built as a completion of `ℚ`.

annotations.label: Hypercomplex numbers

anchor.snippet: They are sometimes called hypercomplex numbers , and are not included in the set of complex numbers

annotations.note: No general `Hypercomplex` umbrella structure; only specific instances like `Quaternion`.

annotations.label: Quaternions

anchor.snippet: introduced by Sir William Rowan Hamilton , in which multiplication is not commutative

annotations.note: `Quaternion R` defined as `QuaternionAlgebra R (-1) 0 (-1)`.

annotations.label: Octonions

anchor.snippet: the octonions

annotations.note: Octonions are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Sedenions

anchor.snippet: and the sedenions

annotations.note: Sedenions are not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Cayley–Dickson construction

anchor.snippet: Each hypercomplex number system is a subset of the next hypercomplex number system of double dimensions obtained via the Cayley–Dickson construction

annotations.note: Cayley–Dickson construction is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Ordinal and cardinal numbers

anchor.snippet: the natural numbers have been generalized to the ordinal numbers and to the cardinal numbers

annotations.note: Both `Ordinal` and `Cardinal` (Mathlib.SetTheory.Cardinal.Basic) are formalized.

annotations.label: Hyperreal numbers

anchor.snippet: Hyperreal numbers are used in non-standard analysis

annotations.note: Constructed as an ultraproduct of real sequences.

annotations.label: Superreal and surreal numbers

anchor.snippet: Superreal and surreal numbers extend the real numbers by adding infinitesimally small numbers and infinitely large numbers

annotations.note: Neither superreal nor surreal numbers are defined in Mathlib.
